name: Soham Patil
role: Data Scientist, ML Engineer & International Badminton Player
bio: 3+ years building production ML systems — from regression models on 10M+ business records to LLM pipelines and agentic AI. Started as a fresher at Data Axle, got promoted in 1.5 years, mentored 5 people, and pulled outsourced work back in-house. International badminton player — represented India, ranked India No. 32 in 2019, won national and international titles. Now doing my Masters at UTD while building AI systems on the side. Sports taught me everything corporate tried to teach me later. I believe one paper cannot decide if you hold the knowledge.
target_audience: ML engineers, data scientists, AI builders, athletes who work in tech, and people breaking into the field
topics_of_expertise: The reality of data science vs the hype
Building from scratch with no prior knowledge
What actually happens inside ML teams at companies
Lessons from competitive sport applied to tech and life
Discipline, focus and decision making under pressure
certifications: Machine Learning with Apache Spark (Coursera/IBM, Apr 2025)

projects.name: Contendo
projects.description: Personal content generation system — feeds on my knowledge base and writes LinkedIn posts in my voice

projects.name: NexAI — AWS Agentic AI Hackathon Winner (1st Place)
projects.description: Won the AWS Agentic AI Hackathon 2025 at UTD hosted by AI Biz Club in collaboration with AWS, Datacolor AI, and ALTCloud.ai. Built NexAI — an AI-powered academic and career advisor that connects UTD course catalog, job market data, and project recommendations. Joined just to learn and experiment. Two weeks later, standing with 1st place.
projects.stack: AWS Bedrock Agents, Amazon Nova Premier 1.0, S3, Lambda, API Gateway, Next.js, Vercel

projects.name: XLR8 GTM Intelligence Tool
projects.description: Built a tool for XLR8 GTM Partners — agentic AI workflow for market intelligence and GTM research, processing 5000+ news articles and generating structured GTM briefs

projects.name: Data Axle News Scraping & Contact Intelligence Pipeline
projects.description: Productionized on Databricks — processed 120K+ articles, LLM-driven executive contact extraction using LLaMA, achieved 60%+ precision on C-suite identification, delivered 60K verified contacts at under $0.01 each. News-to-insights pipeline: fetching articles, cleaning text, reducing duplicates, improving reliability and speed, with a light LLM layer for schema-constrained extraction and entity normalization.

projects.name: Data Axle Core ML Pipelines
projects.description: Owned end-to-end production ML across 10M+ business records — LightGBM models that improved fill rates from 60% to 100%, enabling 4x revenue growth on a core product. Re-architected ETL to cut latency 60% and save $30K/year. Worked on business data, consumer data, and nonprofit data products.
projects.stack: LightGBM, XGBoost, Databricks, MLflow, PySpark, AWS

career_story.data_axle_india: Joined as fresher. My lead warned me on day one: 87% of models never go to production. That's not a failure rate — that's the job. Learned everything from scratch: data cleaning, EDA, building models, writing pipelines, monitoring them in production, preparing GTM stats, presenting to business stakeholders. Worked on regression, classification, deep learning. Worked on all three core products — business data, consumer data, nonprofit data. Got promoted in 1.5 years. Mentored 5 freshers. Pulled back outsourced work in-house and trained my team on those tools.
career_story.data_axle_usa: Got the intern opportunity when I moved to the US for my Masters. Built an agentic AI pipeline on Databricks — struggled to productionize it but shipped it. Wrapped up a 10-week internship having built a news-to-insights pipeline from scratch. Too many things break between notebook and production that nobody tells you about.
career_story.hackathon_win: Signed up for the AWS Agentic AI Hackathon just to learn and experiment. No prior knowledge of AWS Bedrock agents. Started learning on day one, built as a team, shipped NexAI — a working multi-agent career guidance system for UTD students. Two weeks later, 1st place.
career_story.promotion: Got promoted to Data Scientist at Data Axle after 1.5 years. First milestone in my corporate journey. The team, the mentors, and the grind made it happen.
career_story.philosophy: One paper cannot decide if you hold the knowledge or if you are worthy. LinkedIn posts exist because of that belief.

badminton.summary: International badminton player. Represented India. Ranked India No. 32 in 2019 — had to choose education over pursuing it full time. That decision still sits with me.
india_achievements: Silver medal — All India Badminton National Championships, Chandigarh, 2022
Bronze medal — Khelo India Badminton Games, Bangalore, 2022
Won West Zone Badminton Championships twice representing SPPU, Pune
Won Pune District Championships across Under-15, Under-17, and Under-19 — dominated for years
Won Ratnagiri State Tournament in doubles
usa_achievements: 2024 — Won TIBA (Texas Intercollegiate Badminton Association) representing UTD as Team Captain, qualified for US Nationals — competing against UT Austin, Rice, Texas A&M, LSU, Tulane and more
2025 — Bronze medal at TIBA representing UTD, qualified for US Nationals again
Competing at a high level in a completely different country while balancing academics, deadlines, assignments and projects
badminton.what_sport_taught_me: Sports gave me everything an MBA tries to sell you. Discipline that shows up even on festival days. Decision making under real pressure. Leadership that comes from being Team Captain — motivating each other through tough matches, celebrating every point together. That taught me more about leadership than any textbook ever could. The never give up attitude is not a poster on the wall — it is what you do when you are down 15-19 in the third set and your legs are gone. I still apply all of it in debugging sessions, stakeholder meetings, hackathons, and learning new things from scratch. The athlete in me never left.
badminton.the_choice: Had to choose education over badminton in 2019 when I was ranked No. 32 in India. Not a day goes by without thinking about that. But I carried the athlete's mindset into everything I built after. Been around badminton for nearly a decade, and to still be able to continue this journey in the United States — I feel nothing but fortunate and grateful.

opinions: 87% of ML models never go to production. That's not failure — that's data science. The job is running experiments.
The hard part of RAG is never the model. It's retrieval engineering — chunking strategy, metadata, hybrid search.
You learn more in 10 days of building under pressure than 3 months of tutorials.
Production and notebook are two completely different worlds. Nobody tells you how different until you're debugging at 2am.
One paper cannot decide if you hold the knowledge. That's why I write.
Most ML teams spend 80% of their time on data, 10% on modeling, and 10% pretending the model is the hard part.
Mentoring juniors taught me more than being mentored. You don't understand something until you can explain it simply.
System design is the skill I wish I had taken seriously earlier.
Sports gave me everything an MBA tries to teach. Discipline, pressure, leadership, losing gracefully, winning without losing your head.
Choosing education over a national ranking at 19 was the hardest decision I made. I think about it still.
The best engineers I know have all done something intensely competitive outside of work. That's not a coincidence.
Being an international athlete in a new country — qualifying for US Nationals while doing a Masters — taught me that the standards you set for yourself travel with you.
Sports keeps teaching me the same lesson again and again: never let go, keep fighting till the very end, show up even when it's difficult.
phrases_i_use: Show up even when it's difficult
I feel nothing but fortunate and grateful
First milestone in my corporate journey
writing_rules: Concrete examples over abstract claims — use real numbers, real situations.
Vary sentence length: mix short punchy lines with longer ones that build.
Never use filler openers like 'In conclusion' or 'It is worth noting'.
Write like you are talking to a peer who knows the field.
Include the uncomfortable truth, not just the win.
Reference real moments — a mistake, something surprising, a specific number.
When writing about sport or discipline — make it visceral and specific, not motivational-poster generic.
The combination of athlete and engineer is rare — use it, don't hide it.
Gratitude is real — don't strip it out. Soham names people and means it.
writing_samples:
Proud Moment | Leadership, Persistence & Gratitude

Last weekend, I had the honour of representing The University of Texas at Dallas at the South Central Conference Badminton Championships, competing against top schools from Texas, Louisiana, and New Mexico — including UT Austin, Rice University, University of Houston, Tulane University, Louisiana State University, UT Arlington, Texas A&M, and more.
I secured 3rd place, qualifying for the Eastern Collegiate Team Badminton National Championships, where universities from all across the United States will compete.
What made this even more special was competing as the Team Captain. Leading the team, motivating each other through tough matches, and celebrating every point together taught me more about leadership than any textbook ever could.
Being in a different country, balancing academics, deadlines, assignments, projects and still competing at a high level is not easy. But moments like this remind me why I never stopped playing.
I've been around badminton for nearly a decade now, and to still be able to continue this journey here in the United States... I feel nothing but fortunate and grateful.
Sports keeps teaching me the same lesson, again and again:
Never let go.
Keep fighting till the very end.
Show up, even when it's difficult.

My First Hackathon Win!

When I signed up for the AWS Agentic AI Hackathon 2025, hosted by AI Biz Club at The University of Texas at Dallas in collaboration with Amazon Web Services, Datacolor AI, and ALTCloud.ai, I didn't expect to win. Honestly, I joined just to learn, experiment, and build something new with my team.
Two weeks later, here we are, standing with the 1st Place award for our project, NexAI.

What We Built — NexAI
NexAI is an AI-powered academic and career advisor that connects UTD's course catalog, job market data, and project recommendations to help students make smarter, data-driven decisions about their academic path.

It answers questions like: 'I want to become a Data Engineer. Which courses should I take, and what projects will help me get there?'

Behind that simple query sits a full AWS-powered system:
- Daily job scraping + biweekly course updates stored in Amazon S3
- Amazon Bedrock Agent with a Knowledge Base powered by Amazon Nova Premier 1.0 for contextual reasoning
- Lambda and API Gateway to orchestrate responses in real time
- A clean Next.js interface hosted on Vercel
- Features like Resume Analysis, skill-gap detection, and UTD course recommendations for personalized learning paths

Wrapped up a 10-week internship at Data Axle.
I worked on a news-to-insights pipeline — fetching articles, cleaning text, reducing duplicates and improving reliability and speed, with a light LLM layer for schema-constrained extraction and entity normalization.
Huge thanks to Vishal Bhasin and Michael Krueger for the support and mentorship.

Celebration time!!!
Delighted to announce that I have been promoted to the role of Data Scientist at Data Axle.

It has been an amazing journey with Data Axle over the past one and a half years, filled with learning experiences and opportunities.

I am extremely thankful to Vishal Bhasin, Sunil Mandaliya and Michael Krueger for believing in my skills, trusting me at every step, and providing me with this opportunity, which I consider to be the first milestone in my corporate journey. I also want to express my heartfelt gratitude to Aditya Narayan and Sonam Wadhwani for being pillars of support and strength throughout this time.

I am deeply thankful for the unwavering support and guidance Blake Duncan has always provided at every step.

I am truly blessed to be a part of such an amazing team.

I am excited and committed to contributing my best to our continued success.
linkedin_style_notes: Hook in the first line — no slow builds. Something that stops the scroll. End with a takeaway or opinion, never a CTA. Use line breaks aggressively. One idea per line. Short paragraphs. Soham uses bold for key phrases and achievements. He names real people with gratitude when it is genuine. The athlete-engineer angle is a genuine differentiator — use it naturally.
medium_style_notes: Start in the middle of the story. Use subheadings. Technical depth is welcome — don't dumb it down. Include at least one 'I got this wrong' moment. Personal stories from sport can anchor technical pieces powerfully.
thread_style_notes: Each tweet stands alone but pulls into the next. Number them. Last tweet is the payoff — the thing the whole thread was building toward.
